top_profane.text:
This is not the first OnePlus product I've owned, and my first experience with them left an amazingly bad taste in my mouth as it crashed and burned within 90 days and their customer service is pretty much non-existent. Amazon kind of played the, "Hey, now, it's not our problem, it's two days past the return period" game, which, while I get, still was annoying.

So I bought a different phone and it lasted a little over a year before I just started to be a brick. I was moving on to a new job and I figured I'd probably need to actually be able to use the phone to be a phone, so I decided an upgrade was in order. In looking, I stumbled onto reviews for this phone, and while my last experience with them left me screaming I'd never buy another OnePlus product again, I gave this one a chance.

So how is it? It's a phone. It's not a great phone, it's not a crappy phone. It won't win any awards but it also is probably more than worth the price point they are charging for it, and no, I'm not giving it any extra stars because it's "cheap" because it's hundreds of dollars still and I expect any product that costs hundreds of dollars to actually be quality. I'll throw away $10 or $20 on a disposable item, but once it gets much past that, I expect it to work and I expect it to last at least a while.

I could try and get all technical, but I have the intelligence of a particularly slow turtle, so I'm not going to try that. I will try and grade it on the things you probably care about.

Speed: Solid. It doesn't really bog down, and I've not had any real problems with it running any apps, even with a pretty full system tray. It hasn't crashed on me once and I think I've only had to restart it two or three times because it started acting buggy.

Camera: Okay. Probably in the three star range. The previous OnePlus had a way better camera, one which was on par with my wife's Pixel. This one isn't even close to that quality. For me, I'm going to be taking pictures of my dog when he does something goofy, not trying to win photography awards and I'm certainly not going to be live streaming my ugly mug any time soon, so it does what I need it to do, but a lot of users are going to be pissed about the quality.

Touchscreen: Crap. Okay, I'd like to not say it's crap, but I have to be honest, the touchscreen on this thing sucks. Good luck with it being in hands free mode on your dash and trying to hit the tiny buttons on Spotify. It's about a 50/50 proposition with half the time the phone thinking you're trying to swipe up. Same thing on the home screen. I've had some luck with stabilizing my hand and then touching it, but it's still pretty touchy.

Call Quality: Excellent. Yep, it's a phone. It also lets you occasionally talk to someone you probably don't want to be talking to.

Signal quality: Excellent. This is the one point this baby really shines. I have a Samsung for work on the same network as this one and at the office it would constantly drop calls because the signal was so crappy. Same with my old phone. At home my wife's Pixel could be a little finicky depending on where she was in the house, but this guy runs like a trooper in every case.

Unlock Features: Crap, crap, megacrap. It has the stupid little button on the side of the phone that doubles as both a power button and a fingerprint reader and while that seems to work fine, there is a software issue where it occasionally will just wig out and cause the phone to lock up. The lock screen will come up and the lock symbol at the top will unlock but it does bupkis past that. Not world ending, just hit the button again and then wake it up with your passcode and it's fine, but it's still a little annoying to have a feature on a phone that companies worked out years ago flawlessly that doesn't work.

So, is the phone going to solve the world's wars? No. Or at least probably not, there may be a setting I haven't found yet, but it's a pretty good phone for the price. You're not going to be throwing it in your friends' face showing off how flashy it is, but when you take it out of your pocket, it's going to work.
top_profane.title: Look... it's a phone. It's not going to solve world hunger, but it will be a phone for certain!

top_profane.text:
This is not an unlocked phone
Be warned: this particular offering is not as advertised. The descriptions claims that it is an unlocked phone when in fact it is an AT&T branded phone. My phone came from Mobiles International LLC, had AT&T markings on the box, and even included an AT&T SIM card in the packaging. Upon startup, the AT&T logo appeared and the standard stack of AT&T apps were pre-installed on the phone that cannot be removed.

Comparing iOS to Android
If you are moving from iOS, I found the phone to be very comparable to an iPhone 7 in terms of responsiveness and speed. Some elements, like the fingerprint reader, operate faster, and with greater consistency than the iPhone. Almost every app on iOS was available on Android, however the experience was quite often worse. A lot of basic iOS features are either not present in the Android version, or they are a paid extra. This obviously depends heavily on the app and creator. That said, the nice thing about Android is that it is massively more configurable. The downside is that it is massively configurable. This means there is no standard help with common transition questions like, "How do I change the default keyboard?"

Bloatware
Samsung was nice enough to include a slew of their own apps that cannot be removed or even disabled. I was expecting some of this, but was surprised at the ferocity of the demand to create a Samsung account and the level and amount of entrenched crap they pre-install. You have to root the phone to get at some of this junk. Most of the stuff is redundant too. Why give me the Samsung flavor of a browser when I could use Chrome, have my bookmarks synced, etc.? The list of apps goes on; contacts, messages, etc,, all are Samsung variants. Why? Are they better? In a word, no. Pictures, video player, music player, and more are all Samsung flavored. Save yourself some money Samsung, and use the standard Google ones. If the user wants something else, they can download it. Don't force your own crap on the consumer and pretend to be Apple.

Battery Life iOS to Android
Many of the Samsung bloatware apps run in the background and suck down a ton of juice cumulatively. At first boot, after fully charging the phone, it dropped to less than 75% within an hour. My iPhone 7, on the other hand, would be at 95% after a full charge, with typical use, after an hour. After killing many background processes, and disabling the myriad of junk that I could disable, it has become much more efficient, but the battery life still does not compare to iOS. On a light day, I usually end up with about 25-30% battery compared to 65-75% with an iPhone 7.

Battery Life Samsung Android to Stock Android
My 2015 Motorola X Pure (a larger phone) had comparatively next to no bloatware on it, ran pretty much stock Android, and the battery would last all day and even an entire week when left idle. When considering that both the Motorola and the Samsung S7 share the same size 3000 mAh battery, the OS setup on the S7 obviously and seriously impacts battery life. The stock OS on the Motorola performs much better even with it's bigger screen. While you can reconfigure the user experience to be more like stock Android, you need to download all the standard Google apps, such as the keyboard, messenger, etc., and dig deep to find and kill much of the bloatware. In the end, you can't terminate all of it and I believe this is why it doesn't perform as well as the stock OS on the Motorola running Android 6.x or 7.x. Online instructions and utilities for removing this sh..tuff no longer works thanks to Marshmallow/Nougat and the increased security.

Android OS updates
The phone offered an update to Android 7.0.0 Nougat almost immediately after it was up and running. It went very smoothly but I was hoping for at least 7.1, released in December of 2016 (phone was purchased in mid-February). However, AT&T/Samsung have not released that yet. As of this writing, the current release is 7.1.2 but one should anticipate that phone vendors tend to drag behind Google for several months at best. Nougat offers some really nice improvements but also introduces a number of compatibility issues. Fortunately, the developer community seems to be stepping up and repairing things quickly. You mileage may vary depending on the app creator. Some of the enhancements may not even be visible due to the swapping out of the standard Android apps for the Samsung versions (keyboard, etc.). The battery life improvements are hard to gauge due to the aforementioned issues, but I do notice that the speed of battery exhaustion does slow dramatically in the evening or when the auto-battery saver mode kicks in.

Camera
In a word, it sucks compared to the Motorola or even my iPhone 7. Compared to other phones (iPhone 6, etc.), it's okay. It has a hard time finding proper focal points in some situations, like when shooting through glass. It is also very background focus dependent. When shooting a small subject with a complex background, it rarely focuses on the primary subject. Tapping the screen to manually select the focal point seemingly locks on then drifts away a moment later. I would estimate that 50% of my shots are not fully in focus as a result. I never had this issue with the Motorola, or any of my iPhones. Fortunately the stock camera app does allow for quite a bit of control which helps to overcome the focus issues. Video's are very nice and shooting in 4K is great. It is speedy, writes to the SD card quickly and offers excellent playback speed even with 4K video. I'm very happy with the video, not so much with the photography, although with work, it is comparable to the iPhone.

Messaging
In the world of Facetime, and iMessage, I admit I am spoiled. If I shot a short video and sent it as text, my iOS recipients received what I shot quickly and with good quality. Not so on Android. What is sent is a crappy 180px 10 fps video that looks and plays like crap. The whole promise of RCS messaging is currently a nightmare, and it is nowhere close to what iOS iMessage already offers. That said, I can send pictures, smiley faces, etc., so one really has to question whether this is a big deal or not. For me, I can push the video to Dropbox and send the link via text instead. Is it a crappy workaround? Yes. Does it matter that much for texting? No. Do I miss Facetime? Yes. Is there a replacement? Yes, but it requires everyone you know to move to the same specific alternative so the real answer is, not really.

Memory expansion
Well this one blows big time. Samsung, in their infinite wisdom, considered you and me too stupid to know the difference between external and adoptable storage. In other words, you can't easily add more internal storage with an SD card on this phone. It is always treated as a portable storage device. Stock Android simply gives you the choice of how you want to treat the SD card - not Samsung's version. This simply means that you can't move apps to this card and other things you could normally do transparently with adoptable storage. The reason is pretty simple, SD cards are slow compared to internal memory. You can however, store music and pictures, files, and other things like that. You could do that with adoptable storage too, but apparently you aren't allowed to. The hacks I have looked into are haphazard at best and don't fully function. It appears that Samsung specifically crippled the phone to prevent this implementation of a standard Android OS storage configuration option.

Versus iPhone OS
Holy wars asside, I find that this phone is an adequate replacement for a relatively tech-savvy person. My personal belief is that iOS is simply easier to use but far less configurable. I do like the fact that Apple can push out security updates and patches to everyone quickly. With Android phones, you are really at the manufacturers mercy. Security updates and patches may never come.

Versus iPhone hardware
The S7 phone itself is an attractive phone, which is subjective I know. It seems very well built, is almost exactly the same size as an iPhone (I really don't like phablets), and is very responsive and stable. The only hardware downside I have found is battery life is poor compared to the iPhone. Even my age 'old' iPhone 6 does better. However, what I really love about it is wireless charging. Like the first fingerprint reader I used, I ask myself, how did I ever live without this? It is bloody awesome! I purchased the ELEPHAS Fast Wireless Charger from Amazon. Admittedly, it doesn't seem to charge as fast as directly plugging into the Samsung provided charger, but it is damn convenient.

Cost vs Value
In terms of the phone hardware itself, the iPhone 7 is pretty bulletproof but so is the Galaxy S7 phone. I personally opted for this phone due to the cost. A 128GB iPhone 7 with warranty costs $930.68. This phone cost me $520 for a 32GB phone, but I, like many, have plenty of fast 64-128GB SD cards laying around. Is the iPhone worth 45% more (even with a 2yr warranty)? In a word, no. In my family I am supporting five phones and the cost to run them using all Apple hardware is getting to be crazy. Can I move everyone off iCloud calendars, iMessage, iChat, iWhatever? Yes. Is it easy? No. However, when I compare $4,700 vs $2,600 for five phones, one has to start seriously questioning what the real priorities in life (and mobile phones) are. Plenty of other ecosystems offer similar capabilities and while not quite as connected and polished as iOS, it makes one seriously consider the alternatives. Could I move everyone in my household away from iOS? Yes, but it's easier for me to hand an iPhone to a teenager and not worry about them screwing it up too badly (OS wise, not from dropping it) or getting a myriad of viruses on it. However, with proper protection, the S7 is close, and therefore a good value.

Summary
In the world of Android phones, this is the closest phone I could find to be comparable to an iPhone. It is truly a class standard and the myriad of accessories and apps specifically for the Samsung line is staggering next to almost any other Android phone. For example, the new amazing stabilizer SMOVE (avail. on Kickstarter), supports only iPhone and Samsung phones. I also trust that Samsung will be a little more responsive than other brands to OS updates and patches. That said, if the Motorola X Pure had a fingerprint scanner, I probably would have stuck with that, knowing what I know now. I really, really, hate the forced bloatware and the inability to revert to a stock OS. They want to stuff crap into my mouth and tell me it's delicious when it clearly it is not. I also don't like how they purposefully crippled certain stock OS functions (like the SD card format). So I'm dinging this phone a star for poor battery life and a star for the unforgivable bloatware. If this phone cost what an iPhone cost, I would not touch it, but at 45% lower, it's a decent value and safe bet.
top_profane.title: Not unlocked, loads of bloatware, and poor battery life

top_profane.text:
update Nov 4, 2019

This phone is crap if you are a tmobile customer. I have been with them for 11 years and the Xperia 1 is driving me nuts considering that I paid $1040 for the newest of the new that they (sony) said would work on Tmobile.
TMo also said that it is compatible with some but not all of the bands that they use for LTE and 4g.

I cannot stand this PHONE. The phone does not work well, nor does the GPS. After months of traveling by driving 10K plus miles across this USA I can say that this phone is crap on T-Mobile if rated by it's modem alone. My friend has an Iphone 8 and it outperforms this CRAPIERA tenfold with connectivity to cellular, wifi and GPS. I lost signal more times than I care to count while driving across the country. It doesn't matter if I was in Den, Mem, Raliegh, Nashville, NOLA, Shreveport, DFW or on the roads between those cities, this phone has spotty cellular and GPS signal everywhere I have been and in between too. I rely on Google GPS daily and especially while traveling outside of state. It lost the GPS signal so many times. As a comparison I drove from San Antonio Texas to Vegas using my XPERIA Z3, thats android 5.02 in January. Not once did i lose GPS (no audible notification) or the ability to make a phone call (that i noticed).

I cant use my phone as a phone and cant use it for GPS because they are not reliable. My travel buddies iphone 8 never lost GPS or the ability to make phone calls, not once, where this Sony did so constantly. The "GPS SIGNAL LOST" alert blasts through the car stereo which becomes so much more annoying with each continuous notification)

I have traveled a lot since getting the Craperia 1 in July and only now (Nov) have I been able to call TMobile to try to sort things out. Well it turns out that this phone was not designed to work with all of TMos preferred frequency bands. It is set up to work on 3G primarily, being the 1900mhz Frequency in particular. The problem is that TMo and the other majoy carriers are shifting their signals to to a frequency range that is much much lower in the 600MHz (Band 71) & the 700MHz (Band 12) range. If i were near anywhere nearby to a TMo antenna/Tower I would likely experience wonderful service even though it is outdated and currently being replaced. The problem is that I have always had borderline issues with obtaining a strong signal be it LTE or 4G on any of my previous phones while at my house. That is why they (TMo) gave me a cell spot for use at my house. Had i known this phone wasn't fully compatible with TMobiles network I would have not even considered buying it.
Frustratingly even after the restore that it was suggested I do today I am still not able to have a steady conversation while on wifi. I really only decided to do the reset because of all of the below issues that I am also having. I did it in hopes of repairing those issues and hopefully helping with my connectivity. time will tell with the below isssues but as for the connectivity over cellular, NO DICE! it still performs horribly while on a phone call.

I'm going to waste more of my time now trying to figure out why it doesn't work well on WiFi either.

I dont know if this is an Andriod Pie thing or Sony but you can no longer LOCK a text from being deleted.

The phone has died over night causing me to miss my alarm because for whatever reason it does not want to go to sleep all of the time. I have to manually put it to sleep every time. I can't just expect it to do as I told it to in the sleep settings.

I cannot send texts immediately. Memes, Pictures and longer texts take sometimes HOURS to be sent. Whether on wifi or not. - probably has to do with the incompatibility with TMos networks

You cant see the phone while outdoors. it just wont get bright enough and just when you think it is bright enough you will get a message saying that it is about to overheat so please turn it back down. Some will say to use the adaptive brightness or custom feature. Neither of them fix the problem and I bet that those same people live somewhere other than where it gets above 90 degrees frequently and probably work indoors. I So much for Cinema Pro, You cant use it unless you are indoors or it is a cloudy day.

THIS PHONE SUCKS! I WANT MY MONEY BACK DO NOT BUY

they cant fix the message icon issue where it constantly displays that there is a text message..
They can't fix the issue with not being able to Click on an address or phone number in a text and get a pop up with options. The screen just flashes. They tell you to delete the messaging apps cache and whatever to fix the problem. It will then work for an unknown amount of time before it reverts back to not working again. Daily i have to fix this.

The speaker near the bottom of the phone is like 7 times louder than the one by the earpiece and it has much of the EQ range that it needed for clarity in speech. A lot of the sound effects are in stereo so if you have one of the two speakers covered you are going to be missing out on half of the audio. The earpiece speaker and the one by the bottom are not tuned the same. The moment you cover that speaker with either you hand while in landscape or while sitting it on your belly to rest while viewing its in a vertical position you will notice a SIGNIFICANT drop in the volume as the speaker is now blocked and the one by the earpiece is weaker in volume in comparison. The audio then becomes unintelligible.

I'm probably going to return it for another one here REAL QUICK because of the following charging issue: (update I couldn't return it because it took ONE spill and chipped the black finish off the frame) Great design Sony!

I have had the phone for 9 days. Last night while i was in bed reading and charging the phone began to cycle through charging and not charging with the slightest of movement from the cable. I cannot emphasize "slightest" enough. This happened a good 8 times in less than a minutes before i finally said screw it and unplugged the phone to continue reading without being disturbed by its cycling and notifications.

Today i let it die all the way and plugged it in to fully charge it. Yes, this phone charges like no other I have ever owned or seen before. It absolutely blows my mind with how quickly it does the job. I was away from it for thirty minutes and when i returned i noticed that the LED was not illuminated as it should be while charging. I wiggled the phone and got nada. I then tapped the power button to see where the charge was at and to my surprise it began to power on (so i thought) but was actually just acknowledging that it had been plugged in to charge which it then began doing so.

I Cant use the phone while it is chargning without disturbances unless it is in a completely static position. Do I have to check it once i plug it in to make sure the USB-C is seated correctly 9 days out of the box?

Yes, it is very slippery so get a case for it. Dont buy the $12.99 Tyrain ones from Amazon labeled "smooth" red, blue etc. They are **bleep** for cases because they barely cover maybe 60% of the phones edges, besides being equally slippery.
Dont buy the sony style cover for it either. I got the white one. Its not white any more after used some cream on my face and then grabbed my phone. The white case is now all rusted in color, even the clear plastic spine/face cover has become discolored. Ok, so I'll bite the bullet on that one... whatever but that doesn't explain why the plastic cover/face is falling apart two months after purchase (it was $60 from the UK) two of the corners on the "lid" are pealing back and have become sharp and frayed. I did nothing to start its demise. yet Sony has a no warranty on accessories policy.. not even for lifelong customers.

While watching videos on FB with the volume at one level, say max, and then a commercial takes place, when it returns to the video the volume is significantly lowered with no ability to return it to a higher level. No clue if this is Android 9.0, Xperia 1 or FB but it really sux. This only happens while using the phone speakers, not while on Bluetooth. headphones

While watching videos on FB in vertical mode the volume will be at one level but when you switch to landscape mode it will again lower to something less than where it had been with no way of returning to the previous volume besides rotating the screen to a vertical position.
I am an airhead and later figured out that this is because the lower speaker is 7 times louder than the
one by the ear. Once you cover it up or hinder its output in any way you will notice a significant loss in volume / clarity.

I got this phone because I love the Xperia line, period. I didn't get it to play games primarily though the remote PS4 play option was definitely a selling point for me. I haven't played any games that way just yet though i have played PUBG and Fortnite. Both have frozen while loading the games. The phone wont do anything until I manually close the game. hmpf. I'll reset the setting to 30fps and see if that helps as I am currently on 60fps.. you know, the ones they bloated about being so awesome. smh

While playing PUBG yesterday the navigation bar started popping up and not hiding, preventing me from seeing a portion of the screen and subsequently screwing with my game-play because i couldn't access some of the game features. I had to rotate the phone 180 degrees to get to those functions. When i did so the nav bar just went to the other end of the phone. top_profane.text:
"I HATE IT" Might be a bit strong, but not by much. I spent $800+ for this phone. It is pretty, and the rolled edge is something to behold. BUT, once you actually try to use the phone, ugliness abounds. What follows are examples of the poor design choices Samsung made in selection of materials, as well as human factors, not only in the physical shape of the phone, but in the electronics and software.

1) The phone's front and back are made of GLASS. SLICK GLASS. As in, you can't put the phone down on any surface that is not perfectly horizontal. If you do, you will find that the phone has slid downhill while you weren't looking. This phone is so slick it will slide on cloth and leather surfaces. If you put the phone on the seat of your car, for example, it will slide around and underneath anything else that might be on the seat. It's Where's Waldo, 24/7/365.25... The phone is so slick that it is difficult to handle, difficult to pick up, and difficult to operate. STUPID, STUPID DESIGN, Samsung! What the HELL were you thinking? BAD MARKETING! BAD ENGINEERING!

2) The phone is so unwieldy that I have actually hung up on people trying to pick up the phone. I've sent texts while I was still composing the message, because the damned thing is so slippery I have trouble holding the phone and tapping out a message.

3) The text messaging application Verizon/Samsung chose is a piece of crap. It will munge something I've written that is perfectly spelled into something unrecognizable. It has taken a single word I've written and turned it into two- or three-words, which have nothing to do with the meaning I'm trying to convey. When I back up over the trainwreck of jumbled letters and words, the application pops up with what I typed in the first place. Thank you for the extra work you are making me do, Samsung/Verizon! I don't have enough to do in my busy day, already. This lowers my apparent IQ at least 60 points... STUPID, CARELESS design/ implementation!

4) Stupid, inconsistent human factors design in the way the text message keyboard presents lower case on all keys when one is about to type out a message- if one presses the up arrow to capitolize the first letter, ALL CAPS WILL BE ENABLED. STUPID DESIGN/CARELESS SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT/ POOR QUALITY ASSURANCE.

5) More stupid design in the curved edges- one cannot grasp the phone without activating some unintended gesture that sends the text messaging or home screen into a frenzy of activity I guarantee you you didn't intend to have happen, and you may not be able to recover from- deleting text, photos, messages, or applications as you watch in stunned amazement/horror... STUPID, STUPID software development and NON-EXISTENT Quality control.

6) Audio alerts are inconsistent. Sometimes there will be a beep accompanying a received text, but much of the time, there won't be a sound. The volume is up, and vibration is on, yet there's no alert of the new message

7) The little status light in the upper left corner of the phone indicates it's on (blue), there's a text message (yellow), the thing is charging (red), or the battery charge is complete (green). At least that's the plan. Reality is somewhat different- sometimes a message comes in (with or without a beep), but the blue light continues to blink, instead of turning yellow to alert you to the new message. If the phone is plugged-in to the charger, forget about the visual message alert- the red charging light will blcok the yellow message alert from being shown. Same with the green "your battery is fully charged!" state- can't blink that yellow light alternately with either charging or charged state indications, huh? Simple to implement in software, if there was any forethought.

8) Trying to make a phone call is a pain in the posterior! Let's say you are going to call your best friend- you go to the contacts list, search for your friend's name, and when you see it, you click on them. THEN you have to click on the PHONE synbol- does that initiate the call? WHY NO, IT DOESN'T! You get the dial buttons with your friend's name and phone number populated in the number field. Then you PRESS THE PHONE Symbol on THAT page, and the phone finally places the call. IRRITATING! If you are trying to drive a car, it is very distracting to have to continually look at the phone to see why you don't hear the call go through and have a ring tone.

9) Crappy integration of bluetooth headsets with the phone- I have a Plantronix Voyager Legend headset that works pretty well in other contexts. Try pressing the headset button to tell the phone to call someone with your voice. IT DOESN'T WORK ON THE S6 EDGE. BUT, it DOES work on an iPhone... EVEN with Plantronix' APP installed and running. CRAPPY DESIGN, SAMSUNG!!!

10) CHARGING- The phone used to charge up quickly with almost any USB charger, regardless o in the car, or at home, UNTIL a SOFTWARE UPGRADE- then the phone started complaining it wasn't plugged into the charger it came with, and started saying it was slow charging, and I mean SLOW. Really, SAMSUNG? You guys are "engineering" yourselves out of my life.

11) General performance- with four processors, one would expect the phone to scream, especially when compared to other single or dual-processor phones. It has its moments, but by and large the phone is lethargic in its responses to the human operator's touch. It seems to be frequently preoccupied with something internally, and misses gestures when you want it to do something. Many times, the screen timeout will activate when I am actually touching the screen, but does it recognize my fingers are touching the screen and avoid going to sleep? IT MOST CERTAINLY DOES NOT. Again, I feel compelled to call this phone a great, big, smoking pile of CRAP. And, I'm trying to be nice about what I say about this phone.

ONE POSITIVE aspect of the phone is the OLED display is beautiful and striking, but it cannot make up for all the deficits I've noted here.

I've been using a cell phone in one form or another for almost 30 years, but this phone is making me consider giving the phone back and cancelling my service.

About me: I'm an electronic systems engineer and a technocrat, as well as an early adopter. I know poor hardware, software and systems design when I see it. If I designed and produced crap like this, I'd be out of a job. Maybe Samsung is destined for the scrapheap with this sort of poor execution. I know I will make long and hard consideration before I purchase any other Samsung product in the future.

top_profane.text:
So, I purchased this via Amazon Prime. It was actually sold by Fenix Select - Authorized ZeroLemon Retailer, and delivered or shipped via Amazon. This is undoubtedly and absolutely one of the single worse, if not the all time worse, phone case that I have ever purchased. The tabs over the port covers are fine. The grip is great. However......

The inner clamshell casing......what a horrendous joke that is. This is nowhere, and I mean nowhere, near the quality of the major competitor. This piece of crap doesn't even snap together properly. Have you seen the reviews in which people state that their phone moves around in the case. Well Duhhh! I nearly have to hold the pieces together while putting the outer silicon case on, otherwise it comes apart. Once the outer silicon layer is completely on then the inner shell creaks and crackles as the two pieces of the inner shell move around over each other because it won't snap together properly; the inner clamshell casing is absolutely trash and is what ruins the entire thing. Fix that and you have an A+ number 1 case, but with how it is now you have absolute garbage.

Now, once the entire thing is put together and the outer silicone is on, the sides of the case can actually be pulled away from the edges of the phone, so much so that you can see the edges of the actual phone. I mean, there is nothing sturdy at all about it....nothing at all. The build quality of this inner shell is nothing compared to the Otterbox Defender that everyone is comparing it to. Are you kidding me? How the heck can anyone think that it is? A completely assembled 3 layer case (one layer of which is the screen protector?) and you can actually pull the sides of the case away from the phone so much that you can see the edge of the phone? Give me a break folks. And you think this is protective? The only advantage this has over the Otterbox Defender is that the buttons are very responsive and there is no ghastly gap between the screen protector and the phone, and the latter is because this case has a self applied screen protector.

Now, when taking the case apart I don't have to worry about taking the inner clamshell case apart. It literally either comes unsnapped when removing the outer silicone, or it has already become unsnapped prior to removing it. The snaps don't secure anything. With just the inner shell I can snap the two pieces together (if you can call it that) and then just pull the two pieces back apart. You don't have to unsnap the two pieces, just pull them apart. WHAT A PIECE OF CRAP!!!

Hey......Zerolemon guys.....your case is an absolute LEMON. If you want to learn how to put two plastic pieces together like a clamshell, check out OtterBox or the Pelican Voyager. This stuff isn't rocket science folks, and they can at least show you how its done. Don't get me wrong....the Otterbox Defender for the iPhone 6 is a piece of garbage as well, but at least the build quality is there. This ZeroLemon thing, though, is the bottom of the trash heap; absolute garbage. When you can hear the inner shell creaking around with the silicone outer wrap on it as you hold the phone and move it around in your hand, you know there are definite issues.

Now let's talk about packaging for a moment. Like I stated above, this was purchased through Amazon Prime but was sold by Fenix Select - Authorized ZeroLemon Retailer. This came in a yellow envelope in which was a white plastic bag with ZeroLemon printed on it. Inside that was the case wrapped in bubblewrap with the screen protector and the rest of the gear. I guess you get what you pay for here, and obviously I didn't pay for a box, but instead paid for a plastic bag and some bubblewrap for the case to ship in. Now, watching a review of the case on YouTube I know for a fact that the case has a box....so what gives here "Fenix Select - Authorized ZeroLemon Retailer"?

Hey Amazon....are you guys cool with this garbage? Seriously...this thing is so bad that I would actually expect a response from Amazon or Fenix Select. Even for 20 dollars and change I would hope that most people would expect more than what this delivers.

If below sub par is what you are okay with for protecting a 600 plus dollar phone, then by all means this case is definitely for you. However, in my experience, based upon what was delivered to my house, this case was a complete and total waste of 20 dollars. Will it protect your phone? Probably. But the overall build quality of that inner shell is complete crapola trash. It won't protect it from dust at all despite the port covers since there is soooo much play on the outer edges of that clamshell layer that is on the outer edge of the phone. For dust protection your just as well off with a thinner less bulky case from UAG, Trident, etc., and you'd have MIL-STD drop/dust protection or both and much better overall build quality.

All this just because the inner shell is trash. The packaging I could live with, but the fit, or lack thereof, of this inner protective shell is what ruins the entire thing.

top_profane.text:
I personally love tempered glass as a screen protector but because of the curvature on the s8 it's pretty much not possible so this was basically my only option BUT I know it sounds like a bad thing trust me on this, it ain't.

I had an s7 edge and I hated the wet application screen protector because it looked like crap when I got it on with like all these bubbles and they're not debris because it's not hair or fur or tiny particles but they were microscopic air bubbles and the solution spray that remained under the screen and it looked like crap even after I squeegee-ed the crap out of it. I peeled it off and decided to go without a screen protector. Im very lucky that I never got a single scratch or crack.

I've always used Spigen phone cases and I figured this brand has always provided quality items that when I got the s8 the only reputable company supplying a screen protector for this phone was spigen so I gave wet app another shot; spigen haven't failed me before so I had little reason to believe otherwise.

Let me make this abundantly clear, It will look like crap for like a day after you finish the application.

This is how I applied the screen protector

-examination gloves on so I don't risk fingerprints or oil smudges from my fingers.
-alcohol wipes for the phone itself prior to application
-microfiber; wipe that phone down get rid of any smudges
-dust stickers; these things are amazing, use them often and wisely! Regular tape might leave residue and that's a no-no so stick with what this package provides for you
-follow the instructions as directed, moisten fingers and peel the protector off its sheet and spray the adhesive side, then apply (I also spray a couple spritz on the phone itself)
-after it is in the desired position, spray the screen and use the squeegee to squeeze out the excess solution
-for the rounded edges, follow the directions on the package, use some pressure and push the edges down with the microfiber or squeegee. If the edges start to stretch under pressure, ease up that's too much.
-then wipe off excess moisture

you should be left with bits of air bubbles that you should be able to push out with the squeegee. And then there's some spots where it's just stubborn, leave it, it'll go away on its own.

I recommend using a spigen case with this spigen screen protector to avoid things like the screen protector being peeled off by another phone case. I've always used spigen so I can't say what other phone case is compatible with this screen protector.

Another huge perk, there's 3 screens in one package. That's great cuz let's say you really messed up and it looks like crap (I'm pretty sure you can just take the screen off and use re-use it as long as it's not damaged) you have 2 back ups.

Im not careless with my phone I never had a crack before I honestly don't know how people manage it but it happens and I'm just glad it doesn't occur to me. But when you have a phone plan that offers a "trade in at end of lease" for an upgrade, you want your phone to not be scratched or anything of the sort so make sure you protect the phone! Keep it in the same condition as you got it!

5 out 5. I recommend this company to everyone not just this single product.

top_rant.text:
Absolutely LOVE the iRing!! This product has been a game changer for me when it comes to holding my smartphone. I have Rheumatoid Arthritis full blown in both hands so holding on to my phone has ALWAYS been a problem, until now. I'm not an old geezer, so NOT using a smartphone is NOT an option. All the cases for cell phones make my hand problems worse. The iRing creates all kinds of ways that I can hold my phone easily and pain free as well. The fact that I can choose WHERE on the phone to place the iRing is a huge plus in my particular case because as you may guess, I won't be placing it in the "normal" positions of those with normal hands. For instance, if it were already put into position by a manufacturer on a case, it would be of no use to me! My hands are no longer the same shape as normal hands so I don't hold my phone the same way as normal hands. Being able to choose WHERE the location of the iRing will be placed is crucial! Thank You iRing manufacturers for making your product so versatile by giving me the options I NEED! Making this product so simple is the true genius behind the design.
Without the iRing I was having a lot of trouble holding my phone when taking pictures. Since I'm a photographer in real life, trying to take a quick shot with my cell had turned into a nightmare, I had pretty much given up. The iRing has changed that process 100%! I can now easily hold on to the iRing and take my shot, no problems, no more fumbling, no more crooked fingers in the shot!!!
This is one of those products that I will be buying multiples of, simply because I will save them for future cell phones. Have you ever had a favorite product and used the ONE that you bought until it was worn out? Then you go to buy a replacement only to find they are no longer being made?!!! Arg! Not this time, NOT THIS product, at least not me! That is why I will be buying multiples! I will save them for future usage! I will probably find other uses for the iRing too! But for now, it's just on my cell phone.
The only thing I do not like is the hook that came with it. I did try to use it in my car, but it doesn't work so well. The length of the hook needs to be longer, as in stick out more and it needs to have more of a curve to it. In my case the iRing did not stay put on the hook when my car turned the corners. There just wasn't enough of a hook to keep it on the hook!
That is my only negative opinion and I'm not even going to take away a star because the hook is just an accessory. The iRing works better than great!
I thought one problem I might have with using the iRing would be the fact that the ring is metal with a squared edge. I thought it may become uncomfortable for my fingers. Well, I've been using it constantly for more than a month and so far no issues at all. I'm very surprised because my fingers are super finicky about being comfortable, maybe because I can change the position of the iRing by turning it in any direction, which I often do, so perhaps this is the reason the comfort has not been a negative issue. Although, I would probably NOT turn down a rubber, or even cloth cover of some kind for the ring, although a cover of any kind might just get in the way! One of the reasons I like the iRing so very much is because the ring size is so large and the metal is so smooth, this allows my fingers to glide in and out so very easily, which doesn't happen very much with other products these days. Therefore, having a cover on the metal ring just might take away the smooth gliding feature that the plain metal brings. So, I'm still very happy without a ring cover, and I am extremely happy that it is NOT made of plastic! I believe if it were made of plastic I would not trust it so much. Plus, it would look cheap, mostly because it would BE cheap. The way it is now seems to be perfect!
Please DON'T CHANGE IT!
Also the size of ring, the diameter, is quite large. However, because of my weirdly shaped knuckles, I kinda like the extra large size. I was pleasantly surprised to see that the ID, the Inside Diameter of the iRing also fit on my husbands fingers! He has very large and wide digits, and although there wasn't any "growing room" for him, the iRing still slid right on to his fingers! He was equally impressed by this. He also likes the iRing and wants one for his phone too! More reasons for buying more iRing multiples! Which brings to mind what a great gift these will make! Perfect for stocking stuffers! And because of the price, the iRing is a perfect teacher gift too!
I want to add that before purchasing the iRing, I did see the knock-offs. I did think about buying the cheaper version. I ALMOST bought the cheaper version. Afterall, there is a significant price break! I think the knock-off was either $5 or maybe even less than $5!! But there was only two reasons AT THAT TIME which made me choose this more expensive iRing.
Reason # ONE: I definitely do have more respect for the original product. I mean it just bugs me sometimes that the knock-off people creep right in copying someone else's hard work! It's a slimy business being in the knock-off biz, don't you think?!! So for THAT reason alone I tend to lean toward the originals. But then there's still a big price difference, so if I'm going to buy multiples, maybe the knock-off would be ok? See, I'm trying to talk myself into saving money. So then I go to my next criteria, the second part of making the final decision...
Reason # Two: The way it LOOKS.
Yep, the way it looks can often be MY decision maker, there's no doubt. In my line of work, artistic expression is what makes or breaks the final outcome. So I have to be honest and say the cheaper version, the knock-off, well it left a whole lot to be desired, IMHO. Each time I looked at it, it made me think of the Flintstones! Seriously!!! I tried to picture myself using the rougher knock-off version of the sleek iRing, and I kept feeling uncomfortable! It sounds kinda dumb as I'm writing it down, but that's the truth! For me, that was the dealbreaker. But that's because I'm an artist and the way things look have to have a good feeling for me. It's not really an ego thing, I didn't and still don't care about what I looked like using it, no, it's what it would've felt like to me, the comfort of the product. I had no more to go on than the photos. So because of reason number one along with the sleek design of the original version, THAT'S why I chose the original iRing. I am bringing this up because I want to tell the people who are on the fence with this issue. Now that I have the actual product I can honestly say that I am soooo extremely happy that I paid for the original, I can truly say that I will NEVER again have even a second thought about which one to buy again. And I WILL be buying more iRings. Why? The answer is simple. The product, the original iRing is very well made, period. Yes, it looks better than the cheaper version, but that is no longer in my decision making process. Yes, it has "a label" which is predominantly located for ALL to see. That is NOT a reason to buy the original. I have never been a label seeker, and honestly, I'll probably find an artful way to cover up that label. What REALLY matters is that because this product is well made, I'm convinced that the iRing will last much longer than ALL the phones it will be carrying!!! Which is why -I think- they gave us a reusable sticky pad! Long after I'm sick of the phone case I'm using, I will still be wanting my trusty iRing. So peel it off I will (I will find a way! Or probably have someone else do it for me!) but it WILL come off and I WILL use it on my next phone case, and all my next phones down the line, I'm sure of it!
The knock-off does NOT have a reusable sticky pad (I don't think!) Plus, I don't think the knock-off will last. I haven't had it in my crooked little hands to feel how well it is made, or how badly, but that is still my opinion. The bottom line is that the old saying, "You get what you pay for" applies to this product FOR SURE! The iRing is very well made, it does not feel cheap. I'm pretty sure that it doesn't cost a whole lot to have these manufactured, but we're all used to THAT aren't we?!! The big choice is -which one is made to last? I'm quite sure it's the iRing, NOT the knock-off!!! And for THAT reason, I consent to pay the extra price of the original product. Who ever came up with this design deserves to make buckets of money!!! It's one of those things that make me say, "Why didn't I think of that!!"
Have I left out any of the features?
Ahhh, the Sticky Pad! The sticky pad on the back of the iRing is super strong! I haven't tried peeling it off my phone to clean it, but I have had that type of sticky pad before, the kind that rinses clean and it's reusable, so I have every confidence it will work just fine. I think it was a genius move to spring for this type of sticky pad in such a low cost product! After thinking about removing this sticky pad, I actually I don't think I COULD peel it off, I'm trying right now and it's REALLY stuck on the smooth plastic phone case!! Geez, I think I will just leave it there for the life of the case! But in full disclosure, my hands and arms are no longer very strong, so I'm not a good judge on the strength of the sticky pad that way. However, I can confess that I AM quite clumsy at times, and I have had my phone caught between to stationary objects, with my finger still inside the iRing, and my way of freeing my hand and phone from this problem is just to keep pulling on the iRing! Yes, not the smartest way to solve that problem, but that's what I did. The result is that the iRing won!! Each time!!! Yes, it happened more than once. Because I'm clumsy, that's why. So it has now happened three times, and each time the iRing has won, and each time the sticky pad never even budged -I checked! And so I'm sure I will continue to test the sticky pad with every confidence that it is stronger than I am!
Which just goes to show that for people like me, perhaps there is no point in even trying to un-stick the iRing when it's time for a new case, instead I should just recycle the case over to someone else who will use it and enjoy the iRing which is attached!
And so, yet another reason to buy multiples of the iRing because I'm quite sure that when I do change phone cases, I will need a new iRing for the new case!
The point is:
I don't ever want to try to live without the iRing again! That sounds pretty extreme, but it really has changed my entire cell phone experience for the better. As long as I have a cell phone, I want to have the iRing too, no question! I suppose I sound like this product is just too good to be true, and frankly I actually feel as if it IS too good to be true! I don't usually gush over products, especially in a review! But this product feels like it is going to be around for a long time! I actually TRUST the iRing to not let go of my expensive cell phone, which just might be a first for me with cell phone accessories! Let's face it, cell phones and their accessories do not have a great track record! But for some reason, (maybe because it is a simple product that IS well made) the iRing IS different, like I said I TRUST it, and my trust is not given out easily.
So all in all I give it 5 well deserved stars, and I would again like to THANK the makers of this cell phone game changer! THANK YOU for making my life just a little bit easier every single day with your well made, well designed product, the iRing. Thank You.
top_rant.title: The iRing is a game changer for me!

top_rant.text:
As far as looks go, IMHO, This handset is quite a comedown in contrast to its predecessors, the Samsung Galaxy A70 & A71, the fabricators settling for a rather plain mein in pastel colors(the Awesome Violet & Awesome Blue) which might appeal to feminine buyers.
I would've preferred Samsung retain the gaudily flamboyant "glasstic" silver with the prismatic light show. Be that what it may, instead of either Red, Gold, Cobalt Blue, or Purple color I've seen earlier 📱, I went for Awesome White, (at least the metallic-looking color-coordinated bezel looks snazzy!)
I also dig that for the first time in this division, a modicum of dust & water protection up to IP67. In addition, despite my worst fears via reading other reviews, the A72 is merely slightly wider @ 6.7," nor @ 7.16oz(203 grams) not heavy at all and easy to grip despite having a claw-life left hand due to an injury.

Since this is an unlocked GSM handset from 🇲🇽, vs. a 🇺🇸 model, to get it to run from our cell towers in addition to activating a phone number, I had to download a file transfer app, then had to call my carrier's customer support, explaining my upgrade scenario requesting a separate Sim card, then input router style settings about the A72 as well as writing a listing of all the apps on the old phone to download from Google Play to reinstall once the new phone was fully operational on September 30th. Because my J7 2018 was roughly equal to today's Galaxy A02, I was as clueless as "Hogan's Heroes" "Sgt. Schultz" as to what was in store for me once I restarted as a fully operational handset!!
I was genuinely gobsmacked by the brightness, clarity & detail of the display & the potential power of the tetrahedral camera array totaling 121MP, with Dual OIS on both the primary & telephoto cameras, & the 32MP selfie rivals my 2017 Canon Elph 360 which my Facebook buddies took note when they saluted the portrait shot I took! The results were so dazzling that instead of immediately going to the Galaxy Store to download a theme or tmwo I decided to keep Android 11 until new Thanksgiving, Xmas & New Year 2022 show up to festoon on my display. The only thing that irked me on the display was that the screen blacked out in 10 minutes despite having a 5kmAh battery & I have the phone tethered to a Type C-A cable that connects to a PC hub!!
I didn't try the fingerprint display because the facial recognition component failed on the J7 leading me to rely on a number code to control both phones.

The performance of the Snapdragon 720G SoC was lively & assertive for what I do with this phone which is posting on Facebook friends, & stream TV as well as 🛰️ radio broad & YouTube Music via my JBL E35 headphones!

Even though I had desired the 8GB/256GB edition of the Samsung Galaxy A72, Amazon only had it in "Darth Vader" Black which I'm 🤮 of, quite frankly! & e-Bay only shipped to residences which promulgates & propagates the plague of porch piracy that's pinching peeps presents presently!!! therefore, the base 6/128 version will have to do. Because I have with this device, 3x the memory, 4x the space augmented with one of the fastest reading/writing sd cards with double the storage of the A72, tailor-made for a Go Pro Camera meaning I'm ready for ANYTHING LIFE HAPPENS TO THROW MY WAY!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

The fact that Samsung states that will update the OS for three years, as well as security for four, are swell providing another 🏹 in the quiver.
As stated, what clinched the deal for me, in the end, was the Dual OIS camera on the primary & telephoto lenses, as well as the zoom capability that made me invest my moola in the A72 while every phone review & their relative was proclaiming the A52 as the ultimate deal!! Here's why I don't believe the hype about the A52:
Up until July 2017, when I scored the aforementioned Elph 360 which had both OIS & 1080p resolution, like the A72, I hadn't taken a successful picture due to meningitis I had as an infant causing my hands to shake excessively causing me ruined pictures via blurriness, which caused me to forsake picture taking from the 1983 St. Patrick's Day parade until July 2017.
I felt that it's better to have & not need, than need & not have!! If not for the lifelong struggles I would've gone for the A71 which is why knowing the significant financial risk I proclaimed A72 or bust!!!!!!!!!!! despite the unique hurdles I faced getting it, I'm grateful to finally have it!! for it gives me a real chance at a photo album of my friends that I never had before!

As for the 5kMAh battery, It does charges up significantly quicker & last longer, i.e.starting this review @ 15:30 on this Wednesday until 17:56 the battery only lost 10% from 100% to 90% while the J7 went from 85% down to 70% & need a couple of hours just to gain the 15% lost, which meant requiring an overnight charge JUST to go from Empty to Full!!

What prompted this upgrade

In retrospect, had I not heeded the push notifications Samsung was nagging me about "getting a slice of pie!" enticing me to upgrade to Android 9, The J7 was still doing pretty good with the only issue being the lack of OIS on the camera. I've always had an inquisitive spirit since my microdot years ergo I couldn't wait to install it on the old 📱 which I did last March 8th via a unique Hotspot on-demand deal Xfinity had, That was the beginning of the end for the smartphone for apparently, with a mere 2 GB of RAM, "Sweet Potato Pie" was in effect, Sweet Potato Cheesecake, that's how slow the "Phablet" ran & it didn't help that I became addicted to weather apps which slowed the poor 📳 to the point that I had to choose between posting, on Pinterest/Facebook, listen to either radio or TV because either one would cause the radio to stop working & a reset & purge only brought a couple of months of surcease before the dropouts resumed, that was the last straw & with the powerful camera array being too potent to resist with the features too captivatingly compelling NOT TO MASTER & one of the brothers demanding I live as austere as they do so I finally took the plunge and finally got it & so far everything's breaking my way the investment is well worth it for me

What irked me
1. That my carrier while coming up with a free Sim card, prefer to offer modern up-to-date Samsung handsets that are entry-level or State-of-the-art flagships that are beyond my paygrade with nothing to upgrade to except obsolete flagship devices with archaic EVERYTHING!!!!!! Forcing one to look for an unlocked, "gray" international phone that may not work
2. For some inscrutable reason, even though we in America undoubtedly are one of the top markets to ply cell phones, Samsung refuses to offer a US version of either an A72 or A72-5g to run on our carriers, preferring to foist on us The S20\21 FE, Well, with the utmost respect to whoever is the marketing swamis, NOT EVERYONE has the bread to invest in a straitened, S series, nor if it were found to be devoid of unique features, such as a charger, expandable memory cards or a headphone jack for regular headphones, they would think it isn't worth it. I have Bluetooth buds; they are NO PANACEA, my JBL Endurance Peak buds, even when fully charged, could only play 4 hours, then go dead; storage via the cloud is much like cryptocurrency, an overrated hype with both your data & money going right into the ether, void, oblivion!, which is why I have a pair of JBL E-35 old-school headphones!! Which sounds very good as the buds without the diva-type issues the buds seem to have!
I also don't think it's too cool to offer 🇲🇽 phablets THAT CAN'T BE REGISTERED IN🇺🇸!
In conclusion, I'm grateful this wasn't my 1st cell phone; I had a steep learning curve which an 🗽accultured J7 Facebook Interest Group would've mitigated, as it was mastering that one was so tricky that I bricked the 1st one but fortunately, I could exchange it, with this A72 the stakes are much higher being it would be next to impossible to return it, I'm glad, with the help of the carrier, everything is OK This A-72 is worth the money I INVESTED IN IT!!!!!!!!!!!! Thank you, Amazon & Consumer Cellular, for making it possible to own a beautiful, well-endowed handset at a reasonable price!!

Update: A warning to any American owners of this handset A month after this review, I received a couple of letters from Consumer Cellular warning me about the shut off of the 3g section of cell towers by January 31st or earlier. & the only symptom I got from this phone was an inability to download apps from Google Play Store! Because of deceptive advertising from Amazon stating that it was a 4g phone, I disregarded it until a receptionist @ CC gave me this phone's 💀☠⚰ knell into electronic 👿! I blew $535 for the phone plus another small fortune for the customizing "bling" I feel that from this day forward, Amazon should recall these devices to either repair by upgrading them to 4g & return them to owners(which I prefer) produce them for a refund or exchange them for a phone that's comparable to the A72 that can run on 🗽🇺🇸 4g towers, take them off US Amazon! This is my sole phone! & I can't afford to get another one because I have other issues I have to deal with @ the moment! Subsequent US buyers,
top_rant.title: The A52 IS NOT THE 💥 💣 It's an A72 that's about to blow up in a month!

top_rant.text:
there are some things about this device and the manufacturer that really upset me!!!! Sometimes when a product has all the right ingredients, but not enough thought is given to the user's experience of ownership and use, I get to the point where it angers me every time I use it - the Pureview 808 and Nokia are perfect examples of this!

I primarily bought the 808 for its 41mp camera. I value pocketable quiet cameras with lenses that don't extend out from the body. When I first heard about the 41mp with oversampling technology in a pocketable non-extending lens camera that can also be used as a phone and internet device, I thought, "Wow, I gotta have one!!!!!!!!!!". So now, here it is and....

its frustration after frustration with this device and how its implemented by Nokia!

First, the camera takes great pictures and in careful hands it can take fantastic photos!!!!!! BUT for the USA market, Nokia in their infinite wisdom has decided that we USA customers be forced to hear "BEEP", "CLICK" and "TICK-TOCK" for focus confirmation, shutter release and self-timer respectively!!!!!! There is no way to turn these sounds off unless the 808 is jailbroken which voids the USA warranty! European 808s allow the user freedom of choice to turn camera sounds off. So thanks a lot whoever it is in Helsinki, that has seriously compromised the camera's utility for me!

Oh, want to be able to send some of those gorgeous photos to family, friends and clients from wherever you are with your 808? Good luck if your email is on AOL. I have been spending hours trying to make it work, and so far I'm just going around in circles! If anyone out there knows how to do it, please so very please let me know!!!!!!!!!

If you want to get really angry and frustrated, try calling customer service for help on whatever problem you may have with this complex device!!!!!!! You'll be sent on a trip to some foreign land where English isn't the native tongue - Your confidence in anger management will surely be tested!!!!!!!

What really saddens me is that 15 years ago Nokia was "king of the hill"! Oh the idea of owning a Nokia - WOW!!!!!!! Today, my experience with Nokia has banished the warm memories of yesteryear! By buying the 808, I was rooting for the underdog in a world where Apple is playing it safe. Its like it was 1998 all over again when Apple was the underdog with the great technology just waiting to be discovered. I thought Nokia was on the verge of doing the same sort of thing with the Pureview 808 and other upcoming phones.

Come on Nokia!!!!!!!! Get it together!!!!!!!!! Technology can't do it alone. You want to survive and thrive again? Then make the ownership experience enjoyable and fun!!!!!!!!!!

Update 10/15/2012:

It was discovered that the Exchange Activesync email option won't work with AOL. This is the only option for email when no SIM card has been installed. Apparently, more options for email will appear once a SIM card is installed. It is unknown whether AOL email will still work once the SIM card has been removed - that is if AOL email will work with a SIM card!

I then heard about Nokia Configurator 6.3 that is run on a PC while the 808 is connected via a USB cable while running Nokia Suite for configuring options such as email - How complicated can this get?!

Meanwhile on an Apple iPhone 3gs setting up AOL email was a breeze! Simply go into settings to set up mail, press AOL and enter your email address, password, number of emails to be visible in inbox, how often you want it to sync, and you're DONE - SIM card or not!!!

Another update 10/15/2012 on camera usability:

One piece of good news:

I can get rid of one "BEEP" that's involved in taking a photo, by choosing either Close Focus, Hyperfocal Distance Focus or Infinity Focus!

Now for more bad news on camera usability:

When you switch out of auto focus to use one of the above 3 preset focusing distances, you can't lock exposure by pressing the shutter release halfway. You can only lock exposure when pressing halfway on the shutter release while using autofocus, and you must let it focus on what's in the center of the frame.

Fortunately, when you choose a preset fixed focusing distance, you'll see a visual reminder on the settings icon on the top center of the LCD. That way when you move to another shooting situation where a different focusing mode would be desired, you'll be reminded of the focusing mode and it can be changed in time before shooting. Thus botched photos are avoided!

Unfortunately when it comes to reminders, ISO and exposure compensation settings reminders need to be vastly improved! For ISO, "ISO A" is the default. If you switch to ISO 50, the ISO icon will say "ISO M". It needs to say "ISO 50"!!! I want to know what the ISO setting is, not just be reminded that I selected an ISO!!!!!! When it comes to exposure compensation, you're only reminded that you've selected to overexpose or underexpose!!!!! How about displaying "+0.7EV", if that's what I've done????!!!!!!!! If I overexpose by +0.7EV, only a "+" is displayed!

Helsinki Camera Usability punchlist:

1. Allow muting of camera sounds for USA customers.
2. Allow "half press" exposure lock for all focusing modes.
3. Show the actual exposure compensation and actual user selected ISO on the LCD.
4. Allow histogram to be shown at all times if desired.
5. LCD preview will go dark when set at a low ISO if in a dim environment. The photo will be properly exposed. LCD needs to match actual exposure in all situations.

Nokia has been incorporating cameras into phones for a long time. The 808 is the flagship that should show everyone how it's done! In order to do this, the "photographer's cellphone" needs to handle like a camera!!!! Fix these above items and it will be a great little shooter!!!!!!

Update 10/16/2012 - More good and bad:

The good:

When set to 16:9 34MP, ISO 50, the 808 produces an image with more usable resolution than a 16MP ISO 100 fine JPG image from a Sony SLT-A35 w/18-55mm lens set at 18mm f/5.6. The 808 image is more contrasty (lighter highlights, darker shadows), but it has no color fringing or chromatic abberation which is exhibited in abundance in the Sony photo.

The bad:

The resulting photo frequently differs from the LCD preview. In order for the LCD preview to be an effective tool, the preview image needs to closely resemble the photo that's about to be taken! For instance on the Sonly SLT-A35, the playback view of the photo very closely resembles its preview, while on the 808, the resulting photo can look a fstop darker or show a difference in contrast compared to its preview.

Update 10/18/2012:

More camera usability challenges:

Since the exposures from the 808's camera are contrasty (like shooting slide film), you have to nail exposure to preserve the highlight detail without the shadows going "black" too. There are 3 ways with the 808 to control exposure:

1. Use autofocus without tapping the screen, by pressing the shutter release halfway. If the preview looks too light or too dark, move the camera a little towards a lighter or darker area and repeat the procedure until the LCD preview looks right and while holding the shutter release down halfway, recompose and shoot.

The problems with this method are:

The 808 beeps every time focus in confirmed. Not good if you're photographing candidly anything that's alive or if you're photographing a quiet moment that's not to be disturbed. Big problem for USA customers since the beep can't be muted!!! Also the LCD preview of the photo that's about to be taken, can't be trusted either! This is unfortunate, because its the quickest way to handle exposure on this camera.

2. Use one of the fixed focus settings to get rid of the beep and use exposure compensation:

The problem with using exposure compensation as a real-tome exposure adjustment is that the LCD preview and histogram don't change fast enough as you're "sliding" the exposure compensation setting. Another problem is that the histogram seems to reflect the preview image as displayed by the LCD. As stated earlier, the LCD preview is not accurate enough to be relied on for correct exposure. It appears to be that in dim situations with low ISOs, the LCD preview image is darker than the resulting photo, while in bright conditions the LCD preview image is lighter than the resulting photo! Since the histogram appears to track the preview image, the histogram is unreliable too!

3. Use bracketing mode for 3 or 5 exposures:

One would think, "Bracketing could be the way to go then to guarantee that one picture turns out well without any fuss!". WRONG!!!!!!!!!! Besides the fact that a 3 exposure bracket will allow 0.3EV increments and the 5 exposure bracket allows a minimum of 0.7EV increments between exposures, the bracketing function doesn't even work properly! First of all its glacially slow in full resolution - about 4-5 seconds between exposures. Also when a 3 exposure bracket is specified, you get 2 exposures, and with a 5 exposure bracket you get only 3 exposures!!!!!!!!! That's how bracketing behaves on my 808 in full resolution mode - its unusable!!!!!!!!!

A lithe little trustworthy silent candid shooter it is NOT!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

Update: 10/19/2012 - One step forward, two steps back:

The one step forward:

The beep, click and tick tock camera sounds: They're gone!!!!!!!!!!!!

Bracketing can work using 5 exposures!!! You can also set the exact exposure compensation for each of the 5 exposures when in bracketing mode. This is really cool to be able to customize each exposure!!!!!!!

What caused this miracle to take place? CameraPro! Download the CameraPro app for the Nokia Pureview 808 from the Nokia Store.

Unfortunately, this newfound goodness comes at a cost!

The two steps back:

1. When "oh so quietly" using the 808 (its very quiet!) with CameraPro in single shot mode, sometimes at random your just taken photo isn't saved and disappears into a digital black hole!!!!!! If being a "pro" means getting the picture, then the "Pro" in CameraPro is undeserved! This malady occurs frequently! A significant number of photos won't come home with you! This is intolerable!!!!!!!!

Fortunately in bracketing mode, every photo gets saved. If you only have 1 opportunity to take the photo, use bracketing mode in CameraPro, and you'll not lose a photo (at least I haven't yet!).

2. As far as I can tell, CameraPro has no playback mode to look thru my just taken photos. It only provides for a playback of the shot just taken for a timeframe of your choosing and then reverts back to previewing mode.

If I want to be sure a photo has been saved, I must use the Gallery app which has problems of its own! It takes time for Gallery to be ready for you to view an individual photo at times. At times when you try erasing the photo, the erasing doesn't "take" and it has to be erased again!

CameraPro has no AE lock at all, thus exposure compensation must be used. Its slider is sort of finicky and hard to use quickly.

Sometimes CameraPro will confirm focus, but the photo will be out of focus in the area that focus was confirmed. When that's happened, I've switched to macro focus and reshot to get correct focus.

Oh, the lens exhibits a complex distortion. If you're shooting a horizontal composition and the horizon is near the top of the frame, the horizon will dip down towards the center of the frame while out towards the edge, the horizon stays flat. If DXO labs can make a module for the Pureview 808 to fix this distortion, that would be wonderful!

Also the refresh rate for the LCD preview is slow which means that if you're trying to press the button for the decisive moment, you might not even see it unless you look at the scene outside the camera!

So if the Pureview 808 is so frustrating in so many ways for me, one might think, "Why is he trying so hard to make it work?"! Image quality is the reason! If you have fond memories of shooting slide film, the images from the 808 will scratch that itch! There's virtually no chromatic abberation in the images, and I also want to be able to email these "slides" to people right when the images are made! Also it fits in a pocket, and it "can" have silent operation, and it doesn't look like a camera since there's no extending lens, and it has the ability to surf the internet via wifi or 3g and it can place and receive phone calls!

So far the Pureview 808 has the guts of a camera, but not the soul of a camera!

Update 10/25/2012:

When the CameraPro app is used for working the camera, many photos are corrupted when saved. To reliably get a photo out of CameraPro, burst mode is utilized where it is set to take 2 photos. One out of two photos gets lost! Also, there's a one second delay before the first shot is taken. Thus if I reliably want to get a photo of something that moves where timing matters, its back to the default (noisy) camera app!!!!!!!

Speaking of the default noisy Camera app, sometimes the preview display is blacked out - its like shooting blind!!!!!!! You would think that the preview display would always show up when the Camera app is started!!!!!!!!!!

Today, a SIM card was finally installed. Email via AOL then became a snap to configure - go figure! All that was needed were my email address and password. Since the email still worked when switched to wifi, it just seems like such a boneheaded move that Nokia makes it impossible to set up email without the SIM card installed!!!!!!!!

Oh, downloading the "Brightness" app allows the LCD display to stay at a constant brightness at all times. This is a major help for judging proper exposure!

It all just comes across as "Quick!!! Let's throw this stuff together from R&D into a phone while we still can!!! It doesn't matter if it all works in a well thought out way so that one doesn't have to be a gearhead to properly take advantage of it all!!!". Translation: My sister who knows nothing about cameras would be much better off with the cheapest Sony compact camera than with the Nokia 808.

Update : 10/26/2012:

Of course one of the attractions of a camera that comes with wifi and cellphone capability, is the idea of being able to email photos to people. Photos (at least the uncorrupted ones) taken with CameraPro can't be saved for some reason after downsizing them for email distribution! Somehow the photos taken with the noisy default Camera app can be saved after editing on the 808 - When will the madness end?!!!!!!

Update 11/17/2012:

Just got back from a 3 week trip to Florida with the 808. If you like photographing in bright contrasty lighting, such as on the beach or the art deco buildings being illuminated by bright sunlight in South Beach - forget it! The metering is so unreliable under these conditions, you'll miss more shots than you'll get. Using exposure compensation to get the correct exposure is futile, because sometimes it takes a 2 stop compensation to get it right. By the time you get it right, either the moment has passed you by or the lighting has changed. What's really frustrating is that sometimes when the exposure is almost right and the exposure compensation is moved by 1/3 of a stop, the camera flips out gives a completely wrong exposure and you have to start over! This unpredictable behavior makes the 808 a real bear to use in exciting lighting!

On one hand, I want to give this thing up and forget about it. On the other hand, when the planets are aligned the photos can come out absolutely wonderfully!

Update 11/24/2012:

One of the attractions of the 808 for me was to be able to email photos as I shoot them. Given that I like to shoot at full resolution, the process has become less graceful. In order to email photos at low resolution, the photo must be saved as a lower resolution copy before emailing - this is a pain. On an iPhone, one has a choice of resolutions for the email photo which spares one the effort of having to make a low resolution copy for emailing.

During Thanksgiving a few photo opportunities were missed. Sometimes when the shutter release is pressed (thru the Nokia case), no photo is taken. Also when the Camera app is loaded, sometimes the preview doesn't show up on the LCD and other times "play" mode for photos doesn't work!
top_rant.title: I wanted to love the Pureview 808, but....
